data.title: Brookfield Infrastructure Partners: Now Even Better
data.description:
Article Thesis

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (NYSE:BIP) (NYSE:BIPC) is a high-quality dividend growth stock that benefits from macro tailwinds such as declining interest rates and massive Artificial Intelligence investments that are good for BIP's power and electricity assets, as well as for its data center business. Add a nice operational performance and an undemanding valuation, and Brookfield Infrastructure looks like a highly compelling long-term pick, I believe.

Past Coverage

I have written about Brookfield Infrastructure Partners here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in June, around three months ago. I was bullish back then but believe that BIP/BIPC is an even better investment today, thanks to a slightly lower price, the Federal Reserve's interest rate reduction, ongoing massive spending on AI data center infrastructure, and also due to BIP's recently reported compelling Q2 results. With around one quarter having passed since my last article on BIP, it is time to take another look at the company today.

The Connection To Interest Rates

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners isn't a bank, insurance company, or other type of business with a very clear connection to interest rates. Still, interest rate movements impact Brookfield Infrastructure, which is why the company should benefit from declining interest rates. The Federal Reserve has lowered the benchmark rate this September, following a longer pause, and many Federal Reserve board members see further interest rate reductions in the coming quarters. The following chart shows the Federal Reserve's dot plot as of the most recent FED meeting in September:

The chart shows that Federal Reserve members basically all see interest rates declining in 2025, with further declines expected in 2027. Declining interest rates are good for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners in several ways, I believe:

First, lower interest rates make project financing easier, all else equal. When Brookfield Infrastructure acquires or builds out new infrastructure, it usually doesn't finance those with equity only but takes on some debt. Lower interest rates mean cheaper debt, which makes financing projects easier and cheaper, allowing for better profitability.

Second, lower interest rates mean that fixed-income investments become less attractive for major investors. In a low-interest-rate environment, insurers, pension funds, and so on need to seek out higher-yielding investments to boost their returns. This should make them interested in partnering with Brookfield Infrastructure and other real asset investors, which results in more opportunities for BIP, with the same also holding true for other real asset investors such as Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP) (BEPC), real estate, and so on.

Third, lower interest rates mean that individual investors will likely be looking for income stocks – bonds become lower-yielding, all else equal, making them less appealing for retirees and other income-focused investors. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners, with its pretty high dividend yield of 5.6%, could be in high demand – income investors could bid up this stock, along with other income stocks such as REITs, if interest rates continue to decline in the coming quarters and years.

All in all, I thus like the macro setup for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners: Interest rates are moving lower again, and at least according to current FED projections, that will continue going forward, which should be pretty good for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners and many other real asset income stocks.

BIP's Data Center Exposure

In recent months, we saw bigger and bigger data center and cloud computing deals, thanks to the ongoing rise of Artificial Intelligence. This includes a gigantic order intake pace for Oracle (ORCL), Nvidia's (NVDA) recent $100 billion deal with OpenAI, and the announcement of Meta Platform's (META) massive Hyperion data center in Louisiana.

All of these deals and the announcements of ongoing huge investments in data centers and AI computing power are good for Brookfield Infrastructure Partners, as the company should benefit in two ways.

First, data centers are extremely power-hungry, meaning demand for electricity, power generation capacity, power transportation infrastructure, energy storage, and so on will continue to grow. Brookfield Infrastructure's utilities segment will benefit from that, as BIP is a major provider of power connections, power transmission (thanks to thousands of kilometers of transmission lines), and so on. More power demand will most likely mean more demand for natural gas, as burning natural gas is a way to generate electric power that does not depend on specific weather conditions. Via its natural gas assets, such as pipelines, storage, etc., BIP should thus benefit from that trend as well.

Second, Brookfield Infrastructure will benefit directly via its data business. BIP owns more than 100 data centers, has tens of thousands of kilometers of fiber optic cable, and so on. With more and more companies wanting to use more and more data, and since all of that data must be transported, BIP's data business should continue to see strong demand and have considerable pricing power. It should also be easy for BIP to work on new projects, as it is a well-capitalized and experienced partner for companies that want to build out their data infrastructure.

While the focus regarding Artificial Intelligence investments is currently clearly on tech stocks, companies like Brookfield Infrastructure should benefit as well – so far, that's not priced into BIP's stock, but that could happen in the coming years.

BIP: Recent Results And Outlook

In summer, Brookfield Infrastructure reported its most recent earnings results. The company showed compelling funds from operations per share growth of 9%, accounting for currency rate movements, which is, I believe, pretty nice for a high-yield company like BIP that isn't valued at a high valuation at all.

Revenue growth was a little lower than that, at 6%, but Brookfield Infrastructure Partners benefitted from operating leverage – costs didn't grow as much as revenue, which is why funds from operations grew a little more than revenue. Over the last five years, Brookfield Infrastructure managed to grow its funds from operations per share by 14% per year on average, which is very compelling, but I believe that growth in the future will likely be a little lower – the starting point is now considerably higher than it was five years ago, making maintaining a pretty high growth rate complicated.

Still, even if BIP only grows its funds from operations per share by 8% to 10% per year going forward, that would still be highly compelling, I believe. Investors get a dividend yield of 5.6% at current prices, so in combination with a high-single-digit funds from operations growth rate, a mid-teens annual return would be possible. Even if BIP were to only grow its funds from operations and its dividend by 4% to 5% per year going forward, annual returns would likely be in the 10% range, assuming BIP's valuation does not move a lot from where it is today. That would be a relatively weak growth rate, considering the past growth and the aforementioned tailwinds from AI, interest rates, and so on.

I thus believe that total returns, including BIP's dividend, should be at least 10% per year over a multi-year time frame, possibly significantly higher if BIP is able to achieve a growth rate of close to 10% and/or if Brookfield Infrastructure Partners' valuation expands from where it is today – BIP trades at just below 10x FFO.

Takeaway

BIP got a little cheaper in recent weeks, despite being a beneficiary of both declining interest rates and the ongoing huge investments in AI, data centers, and so on.

At current prices, the stock is priced for a pretty low growth rate, which seems unrealistic to me. I believe that over a multi-year time frame, returns should be compelling, including thanks to a high starting yield of 5.6%.

data.title: ARKK: Trading Into The Wind
data.description:
Introduction

The ARK Innovation ETF (BATS:ARKK) needs little introduction, although I wrote an educational article on ARKK back in June for anyone who wants the basics. I also wrote the educational articles on Seeking Alpha for ARKF, ARKG, and ARKX too; you can find those on my profile by searching the tickers.

Today, I am giving my annual update on ARKK, since my last opinion article, "The Shotgun Approach Isn't Working," back in May 2024. Since then, it has done very well, especially during the most recent bull run following the April crash.

It's all about timing and perspective, though. That's one of the perks (detractors?) of the shotgun approach.

Sometimes you hit big, sometimes you miss.

Your returns with ARKK will highly depend on when you buy and sell the fund. Remember the long-term trend of this fund, and the past bubble that still haunts it.

My caution around investing in innovation has turned out prudent, not just with our knowledge at the time, but overall. Even buying in at the start of the AI arms race (release of ChatGPT) wasn't enough to outcompete the Nasdaq 100, although how much of this next chart is just measuring how much NVIDIA (NVDA) exposure each fund has. That's even more true for ARK's private market focused mutual fund, the ARK Venture Fund (ARKVX), which underperformed as well.

Trading Into the Wind

If you own or follow ARKK, you should watch this website, which tracks the trades of the various ARK funds, including ARKK. They take ARK's briefings of the trades they do each day and aggregate them, along with the daily holdings accounting, on one webpage.

That's because ARK trades daily, actively raising and lowering their stakes in different stocks across their funds. This is one of the core issues I see for ARK; they trade too much for their own good. I don't have the data, but I would love to see how their past picks from 2021 would've performed today if there was no active trading and moving around.

What prompted me to look at ARK was an interesting thing that rarely happens: new stocks were added to the portfolio. What ARK invests in does change, but most daily traded is them trading shares of stocks they already own. When they add or remove holdings entirely, it's an interesting development. Sometimes, it brings small and mid-cap companies to my attention for further research. Sometimes, it's just another red flag — like this one was.

If you're a regular reader of mine, you may know that I am fairly bearish on Chinese markets. So it was absolutely a red flag to see the newest additions to ARKF and ARKW in the last few days.

Context: the 100% being the "% of position" means that this buy started the position. That is also indicated with the star by the date.

When new stocks trickle into multiple sister funds, it's only a matter of time until they make their way into the flagship. Lo and behold, Cathie is starting to add more Chinese stocks, starting with Alibaba (BABA) and Baidu (BIDU), a few days later.

Worse, they are selling their holdings in strong firms like AMD (AMD) and speculative bets that have paid off like Tempus AI (TEM).

In fact, they have been selling TEM consistently since their last buy in July. These are just from ARKK and do not include all the selling from ARKG.

I won't get into the specifics of why I don't like Chinese stocks here, but I recommend taking a look at this article from my colleague Julia Ostian on BABA and why it's not a buy after this last run-up.

My ultimate point is that this is another rotation and change in direction for ARKK, and likely a more permanent one given that the stocks are also being bought in funds like ARKF and ARKW. It is a direction I am not fond of, and do not see much promise in, although I was already fairly bearish on ARKK's overall premise of investing in innovation. The "shotgun approach" has to evolve, and now it is evolving to include more Chinese exposure and less US frontier tech mid-caps. That's not a move that inspires confidence in me, and it is not budging my rating on ARKK, regardless of its recent successes. Again, back to timing being everything with this fund, because of its volatility.

Does Innovation Bring Riches?

The second core issue is that technological innovation tends to be deflationary, and as such may not lead to actual, realized market returns. You can change the world, invent a technology that revolutions a field, and fail to deliver on the valuation multiple speculators have bought your company up to. There is a large gap between real-world performance and market returns, and the gap gets wider and wider the smaller the firm — remember that ARK specializes in mid-cap firms, usually, but are trending toward large-caps lately. I worry that investors will not see the kinds of actual returns that bullish forecasters like Cathie Wood, and the ARK Invest team more broadly, make.

Remember the ARK Invest 2020 prediction of Tesla (TSLA) at $6,000 by 2025? I mean, she still has some time, but it's definitely unlikely. While Tesla was still a good investment five-years on, it's nowhere near the level of return that was promised.

I worry investors' FOMO and greed, which make us make poor investment decisions, are triggered by these kinds of projections. They make us eager to jump in too, especially given how charismatic Cathie and her team can be at times on CNBC or their YouTube channel.

Conclusion

Ultimately, my position on ARKK has not changed during this last run-up. They are still a poor way to achieve high-beta investing, and are even poorer as a proxy for a venture capital position. At least the ARK Venture Fund is trying something different with the private equity exposure, but both it and ARKK have underperformed over the long run because of two core issues that they have still not shaken and seem unlikely to shake given the current trends:

  1. They trade too much and are investing in new stocks that I do not care for.
  2. They regularly overestimate the likely returns of the firms they invest in.

For these reasons, ARKK remains a fund that I do not recommend. There are other options, and I think investors wanting exposure to frontier technologies are better served by the ARK sister funds that focus on a specialty or with other frontier-tech funds that take a more passive approach, like QTUM and AGIX.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Amprius Technologies: More Euphoria Needed To Push This Futuristic Battery Maker Higher
data.description:
Amprius Technologies (NYSE:AMPX) is building batteries of the future. They are faster charging, have a greater energy density for longer operational times, and are safer to use. I last covered AMPX in 2023, highlighting the ticker as a possible speculative buy on the back of the latent value of its batteries against multiple billion-dollar total addressable markets, albeit flagging the risk of buying at too high a valuation on what were then still anemic revenues. The Fremont, California-based company has been on a sustained run of purchase order wins since then, ramping up its revenue profile and cementing the product market fit of its batteries. AMPX generated fiscal 2025 second quarter revenue of $15.07 million, up a remarkable 349.9% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $2.65 million on consensus estimates.

AMPX keeps on stacking up purchase orders. The company recently announced a $35 million follow-on order for its SiCore cells from a UAS manufacturer after receiving a $15 million purchase order from the same UAS manufacturer back in February. The order wins, and hyper year-over-year revenue growth figures have sparked euphoria around the common shares, pushing them up 284% year-to-date. This means a market cap of $1.4 billion against trailing 12-month sales of $44.8 million for a 32x sales multiple. Bulls would be right to highlight that revenue growth is ramping upwards fast, and the current pace would almost certainly see this multiple cut in half by next year. AMPX is scaling up and not currently profitable; sales present the most relevant way to assign a valuation to its common shares.

Purchase Orders And Drone Executive Order

Is AMPX still a buy? It depends. Peer speculative futuristic battery company Enovix (ENVX) trades on a much higher $2 billion market cap despite a revenue profile that is materially more constrained than AMPX. ENVX generated revenue of just $7.5 million for its second quarter. While both companies are not explicitly chasing the same end markets, there are overlaps. Hence, on a comparative basis, AMPX somewhat presents better value than ENVX, with a revenue trendline that's ramping up and purchase orders that continue to come in. However, it also opens up sentiment as a critical component of the company's valuation. The market has, to a large extent, valued ENVX higher based on vibes or stronger animal spirits. These have been raging through 2025 on the back of AI hype, eVTOL momentum, and strong defense spending on drones. 2025 for AMPX has seen the company expand its manufacturing capacity through a contract with a South Korean battery manufacturer. Sentiment can ebb and flow, and AMPX could be caught on the other side of this if it switches to become negative.

A wide-ranging drone-focused executive order in June that pushed to fast-track U.S. drone manufacturing operations, cut red tape to make it easier for the military to buy drones, and established an Electric Vertical Takeoff ("eVTOL") pilot program that has also underpinned a ton of the euphoria in the sector. AMPX has built itself a niche in supplying its batteries to the drone industry, with its most recent win a few weeks ago to supply its SiCore cells to power a fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicle being developed by Danish drone manufacturer Nordic Wing. AMPX focuses heavily on applications in the drone industry, with the nascent eVTOL industry and fixed-wing electric aircraft being pursued by management. eVTOL stocks have been on fire this year, with Archer Aviation (ACHR) up over 200% over the last year and Joby Aviation (JOBY) up by a similar amount.

Liquidity And Cash Flow Risks

AMPX is in the process of extending its liquidity position. This was $54.2 million as of the end of the second quarter, against negative cash from operations of $4.3 million. This means a cash runway of just over three years, with the company raising $9.8 million from the sale of common stock during the quarter. AMPX has placed its monthly cash run rate at around $2.5 million to $3 million per month and had $46.7 million left on its at-the-market facility as of the end of June.

Conclusion

AMPX has built itself a position at the intersection of a number of fast-growing markets. The growth in defense spending in response to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, the executive order to boost U.S. drone competitiveness, and market enthusiasm for EVTOLs have all aggregated to significantly ramp up the revenue profile of AMPX. The company valuation at 32x trailing 12-month sales is expensive, but with revenue growing by triple digits year-over-year, it's not hard to see why some investors are willing to pay any price for AMPX common shares. However, the company is currently guiding to burn through cash at a rate of roughly $2.5 million per month and will be dependent on selling shares to bolster a liquidity position that had dropped, over the summer, to its lowest position since the ticker went public. I'm rating AMPX as a hold.

data.title: REITs For A Rate-Cut Rebound: My Top 5 Picks
data.description:
Is Real Estate Ready For A Rebound?

Residential and commercial real estate companies and real estate investment trusts (REITs) could stand to gain from the latest interest rate cutting cycle, which began earlier in September with similar cuts expected for October and February. Lower mortgage rates make houses more affordable and are likely to spur homebuying, while lower borrowing costs could prop up commercial property values, leading to more sales and lending.

In a recent article, I highlighted how the 25 basis point cut may boost some sectors, offering consumers an opportunity to finance at lower rates. The commercial side of real estate may see the benefits of lower rates much more quickly, unlike home mortgages, which do not track the Fed’s moves directly. Much of commercial real estate is financed with shorter-term or floating-rate loans, which are more sensitive to interest rate cuts.

In the wake of the 2022 hikes, commercial property values fell over 20%, sales sank, and banks stopped lending. After the Fed announced the initial cut on September 17, real estate giant CBRE revised its forecast upward, citing lower borrowing costs. CBRE now projects commercial real estate investment volume to increase by about 15% in 2025 - up from its earlier projection of 10%.

Falling short-term rates alone may not be sufficient for a full recovery if the 10Y Treasury yield remains elevated, keeping long-term financing costs high. Add to that risks and uncertainties from tariffs, stubborn inflation, and a softer labor market. That’s why investors should focus on REIT fundamentals - stocks with consistent operating results, reasonable valuations, and reliable dividends.

REITs Primed For A Rate-Cutting Cycle

Although real estate has underperformed the market in the past year, lower interest rates could spur capital inflows. According to CenterSquare investment strategist, Uma Moriarity, institutional investors have been waiting for this type of easing monetary policy environment to deploy assets into real estate. Moriarity also said that, historically, REITs tend to outperform core private real estate once the Fed starts a rate-cutting cycle. In fact, REITs had begun showing momentum ahead of the Fed rate cuts, with the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index rising 3.3% in August.

Health care has been the highest performing REIT sector year-to-date, with a total return of 21.3% – followed by diversified and gaming. Despite a rough market, REIT operating performance in Q2 2025 remained resilient, as they continued to deliver year-over-year growth in net operating income (NOI) - led by industrial REITs (+23.6% YoY).

In addition to gaining a foothold in a sector that could benefit from interest rate easing, REITs can be a defensive haven when the market is volatile, due to their stable dividend income, tangible asset backing, and inflation-hedging properties. With these objectives in mind, SA Quant screened for high-yield REITs with exceptional fundamentals and found five poised to perform in a low-rate environment.

Top 5 REITs

SA Quant identified five Strong Buy commercial REITs that own a wide variety of properties, including hotels, casinos, senior housing, and industrial warehouses. The stocks on the list have outstanding collective investment fundamentals, robust FFO generation, and high earnings growth potential. The stocks also offer safe and high-yielding dividends, providing investors with downside protection in a volatile market.

1. Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.10B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 4 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 15

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

Host Hotels & Resorts, the only S&P 500 lodging REIT, has ridden the post-pandemic leisure and travel rebound to deliver strong growth in revenue, FFO and AFFO - while offering investors stability with a reliable and growing dividend. The top quant-rated Hotel & Resort REIT, HST, has a portfolio of 80 hotels under major brands like Marriott, Hyatt, and the Four Seasons - concentrated in high-demand markets. By consolidating its focus even further on higher-end properties in recent years, Host has benefited from substantial out-of-room revenue from both guests and non-guests.

Higher room rates across the portfolio and greater spending on food, beverage, and services drove Q2 revenue, up 8.2% year-over-year to $1.59B, and helped HST beat earnings for the sixth straight quarter. Healthy transient leisure demand and the continuing recovery in Maui more than offset a decline in group demand, while improvements in room rates offset rising wage expenses. HST lifted full-year EBITDA and revenue guidance, citing outperformance in H125, hotel operational improvements, and expected growth in Maui.

HST’s FFO has surged by a CAGR of ~47% in the past 3 years, driving an A Growth Grade, while exceptional interest and dividend coverage ratios underpin a quality profitability score. HST also offers a stable and rewarding dividend, sporting a yield of 5.18%. Impressive payout ratios support the company’s ‘A’ Dividend Safety Grade while a 3Y CAGR of 115% contributes to a top-notch Dividend Growth Grade. HST’s dividend consistency grade took a hit after the company suspended payouts in mid-2020, preserving cash as it dealt with the fallout from the pandemic. Host’s dividend has grown steadily since it was reinstated in early 2022.

HST is trading at an attractive valuation – with a price-to-FFO of 8.7x – representing a 35% discount to the sector’s 13.4x. HST provides investors with a great opportunity to play the high-end travel boom, backed by premium asset positioning, improving momentum, and a high-quality dividend – all at an attractive price.

2. National Health Investors, Inc. (NHI)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.77B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 7 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 17

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Health Care REITs

An aging population and surging demand for senior housing have created powerful tailwinds for National Health Investors, Inc. (NHI), SA Quant’s top-rated Health Care REIT. With senior housing representing two-thirds of NOI, National Health Investors is positioned to benefit from long-term demographic trends. The 85+ population growth rate is expected to accelerate to a CAGR of 1.9% by 2030 and 3.5% in the following decade. On the supply side, inventory growth has dropped below 1% while new starts in Q2 2025 were the lowest level ever recorded – and 64% below the historical average.

Boosted by these dynamics, NHI delivered better than expected Q2 results, with NOI surging nearly 30% to $3.8M, primarily due to increased revenue from higher occupancy levels and revenue per occupied room. Excellent year-over-year growth in its Senior Housing Operating Portfolio, or SHOP, and continued collections on deferral repayments also drove outperformance. Looking ahead, NHI said it has $750M in liquidity and capital resources to continue executing on a $350M pipeline - which is nearly entirely focused on senior housing.

Improving momentum in the past 3 months, along with rising earnings and upward EPS revisions, has bolstered NHI’s Strong Buy Quant Rating. Robust AFFO is driving NHI’s ‘A’ Profitability Grade. NHI’s FFO growth has improved in the last 12 months and is projected to rise steadily in each of the next three years, according to consensus estimates.

NHI offers a safe and consistent dividend, yielding 4.63%, with 33 consecutive annual payouts. Growth has lagged, but NHI increased its dividend for the first time in four years - a payout of $0.92 per share will be distributed in Q3. NHI is trading at a premium to the sector with a P/FFO of 16.50x – but positive tailwinds, a safe dividend, and stellar margins, still make it a Strong Buy stock.

3. Xenia Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (XHR)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.46B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 8 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 2 out of 15

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

XHR has crushed the real estate sector in the past three months, up more than 15%, boosted by quality financial results across its 30-hotel portfolio and three demand segments, which include Group (~35% of revenue), Corporate Transient (~40%), and Leisure Transient (25%). XHR’s Q2 revenue of $287.58M (+5.4% YoY), beat expectations by $14.23M, and adjusted FFO per share rose 9.6%, resulting from strong group business demand, a recovery in corporate transient, and normalization in leisure.

Xenia’s Q2 performance was driven by sturdy revenue from the recently renovated Grand Hyatt Scottsdale Resort, substantial food and beverage sales portfolio-wide, and outsized gains in its highly-profitable catering business. XHR has delivered amazing FFO growth in the past three years, up by a CAGR of 78% to outperform the sector. And XHR’s outlook is impressive, with Wall Street analysts projecting earnings to grow by 9% in FY25.

Strong reinvestment and FFO dividend coverage ratios, are driving a solid profitably grade. Trading at 8.3x FFO, XHR is at a significant discount to the sector for a ‘B’ Valuation Grade. XHR offers a safe dividend, yielding 3.89%, that has delivered long-term growth. With an attractive price, outstanding growth, and accelerating momentum, XHR is another Strong Buy hotel REIT for your portfolio.

4. STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 10 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 13

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Industrial REITS

#1 among quant-rated Industrial REITs, STAG boasts 600 properties in 41 states - totaling 118.3M square feet - and has seen positive momentum after it beat earnings for the eighth straight quarter in Q2. STAG has delivered substantial growth and profitability in the past year as conditions improve in the medium-sized warehouse market.

STAG has already leased 90.8% of the square feet it expected to lease in 2025, while achieving cash leasing spreads of 24.5% in the second quarter. STAG has seen a recovery in demand and tightening supply as trade tensions dissipate and businesses look to diversify supply chains, which CEO William Cooker elaborated on in the earnings call:

In the first quarter, news related to the global trade war drove significant market volatility. Broadly speaking, today, the theme has shifted to a general desensitization to tariff headlines. We are witnessing businesses continue to grow and make corporate decisions in an uncertain environment, a change to the broad pause seen in the past 12 months. While it's certainly not business as usual, users cannot delay space decisions in perpetuity, and supply chain diversification remains a priority for many companies.”

STAG has posted steady growth in operating revenue and return on equity, showcases impressive profit margins, and offers a safe and consistent monthly dividend with a yield of 4.30%. The stock is trading in line with the market with a 13.81x P/FFO ratio, but improving earnings growth projections and strong all-around investment fundamentals offer great exposure to a recovering industrial REIT market.

5. VICI Properties Inc. (VICI)

  • Market Capitalization: $34.01B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 11 out of 176

  • Quant Industry Ranking: (as of 9/25/25): 1 out of 12

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Other Specialized REITs

VICI’s stock slid after Las Vegas reported a sharp drop in visitors in July, creating a potential buy-the-dip opportunity. VICI generates significant revenue from the Las Vegas strip – where it owns three iconic hotels – Caesars Palace, MGM Grand, and The Venetian. However, VICI also derives revenue from about 90 other experiential assets in the US and Canada. VICI is now trading at a 12% discount to the sector, at 11.7x FFO, which is attractive given the stock’s exceptional collective investment qualities, high earnings growth upside, and sturdy dividend yield.

Moreover, the rate cut could benefit VICI by lowering refinancing costs, making it even more of a value play, according to SA Analyst Steven Fiorillo:

They have world-class assets that could increase in value as property values increase and rates decline. VICI could also further reduce its carrying costs by refinancing additional debt at lower rates and improving its margins as less capital flows toward interest expenses… As the year progresses, I think that shares of VICI can continue to move higher while rewarding investors with a dividend yield that exceeds risk-free assets.

VICI’s FFO margin of nearly 70% is helping fuel its A+ Profitability Grade, while year-over-year and long-term growth in AFFO and FFO are crushing the sector. VICI’s Dividend Scorecard is solid across the board - with B’s in safety, growth, yield and consistency. Showcasing a yield of 5.64%, VICI’s dividend has grown by a CAGR of 7% in the past five years. The gaming REIT has delivered six consecutive years of dividend payments and growth since going public in 2018.

VICI is the final pick on our diverse list of Strong Buy REITs - a great stable of stocks that possess excellent underlying investment fundamentals, dividends you can depend on, and exceptional earnings growth upside relative to the real estate sector.

Conclusion: Top 5 REITs Poised For A Rate-Cut Rebound

The real estate sector stands to gain from interest rate cuts, with lower mortgage rates likely to stimulate homebuying, as lower borrowing costs stabilize commercial property value. REITs tend to perform well during interest-rate cutting cycles, and experts say institutional investors have been waiting for this type of monetary policy environment before deploying assets into real estate. SA Quant identified five commercial REITs with Strong Buy Quant Ratings across a wide range of industries, which have generated strong FFO. Our stock picks showcase outstanding investment fundamentals, impressive upside growth prospects, and offer safe and high-yielding dividends.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like. Alternatively, Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing, leveraging Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stock picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

For users looking for a more active approach to their portfolio, the PRO Quant Portfolio (PQP) offers a data‑driven strategy that rebalances weekly using Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant system. You’ll get clear buy, sell, and rebalance alerts within a fixed‑capital, equal‑weight framework. Seeking Alpha PRO subscribers also receive exclusive access to unique undercovered stock ideas, real-time upgrades and downgrades, and rigorously vetted short ideas in addition to PQP’s outstanding stock research ideas.

data.title: Analyzing A New Direction For The Federal Reserve
data.description:
Interest rates impact every investment so we keep tabs on all things related to the Federal Reserve. The forward Fed appears to be quite different from the Fed of recent years, so this article will discuss the economics behind the potential new Fed ideology. One can never fully predict interest rates, but a greater level of understanding increases one’s odds of getting the direction right.

Miran is an outlier, but maybe not for long

The newest Fed appointee, Stephen Miran, arrived with a bang, calling for a 50 basis point cut and being a true outlier on the dot plot. His projection for the midpoint of the Fed Funds Target range at the end of 2025 is fully 75 basis points below the majority.

With Powell’s term ending in May and the lingering possibility of Lisa Cook being removed, there could be up to 2 more like-minded individuals joining the Fed, one of whom could be in the chair position.

Thus, it behooves us as investors not to dismiss Miran as an outlier but rather to study his methodology as it could have an increasingly large impact on the Fed.

Those who follow my work know that I prefer middle of the range interest rates and believe a 10-year Treasury floating somewhere in the vicinity of 3%-4.5% is healthiest for the economy. As such, I do not necessarily welcome the idea of a radically lower Fed Funds rate, but it is still worth understanding the economic ideas behind it.

On September 22nd, Miran gave a very detailed speech at the New York Economic Club which revealed quite a bit about his differing perspective on rates. Specifically, his views diverge on inflation, R* and the economic output gap. These are summed in the table below and then we will discuss the economic concepts behind them.

Miran expressed his fondness for the concept of the Taylor rule which he believes in using to inform but not dictate decisions. The Taylor rule formula looks intimidating, but most of the difficulty is in the notation with the underlying concepts being rather straightforward.

We will reference the Taylor rule as notated on Wikipedia because it uses the same notation as Miran’s table above.

In plain English, this says:

The Fed Funds Target Rate equals inflation plus the equilibrating real interest rate and then 2 adjustments for actual inflation relative to target inflation and actual output relative to potential output.

Miran suggests that inflation is actually lower than what is currently being measured by the Fed.

“Because measured inflation incorporates rental inflation with a lag, it has remained elevated above market rents for several years, infamously "catching up." While an index of all-tenant rents took time to incorporate the spike in new rents in 2021 and 2022, that gap subsequently closed, indicating the catch-up is complete. Now, new rent indices show this inflation is well below all-tenant inflation, around 1 percent annualized. Rents for all tenants will fall toward this low rate as people moving or renewing leases sign at current market rates, and I expect CPI rent inflation will decline from its current level of roughly 3.5 percent to below 1.5 percent in 2027. This implies a roughly 0.3 percentage point decline in total PCE inflation; by early 2028, I expect that effect to grow to a 0.4 percentage point decline. Per a Taylor rule, that works out to an appropriate fed funds rate roughly half a percentage point lower than current shelter inflation would imply.”

The lag in how rental inflation is recorded has been discussed by many, including Jeremy Siegel. As we observe the rental rates of apartment REITs, it is clear that rents are basically flat year-over-year, so I do think there is truth to the standard measure of rental inflation being significantly lagged.

Using a more current number for rental inflation, Miran thinks inflation is about 40 basis points lower than the Fed’s number, which would imply a 50 basis point lower Fed Funds target rate per the Taylor rule.

The majority of Miran’s adjustments are to R* itself. As a reminder, R* is the equilibrant real interest rate – essentially the interest rate in excess of inflation that the market demands on short-term capital.

R* is a necessary concept that seemingly every Fed official uses, but at the same time, it is unobservable and thus somewhat nebulous. Inflation gap and output gap can be largely measured so it is easier to base policy on those using a somewhat constant R*. In recent years, the Fed has been somewhat hesitant to make adjustments directly to R*.

However, there is precedent for it. A 2016 Fed paper discusses some of the macro factors that impact R* with one of the largest being population. Per the Fed paper:

“We find that demographic factors alone can account for a 1.25 –percentage-point decline in the equilibrium real interest rate in the model since 1980. [ ] The model is also consistent with demographics having lowered real GDP growth 1.25 percentage points since 1980, primarily through lower growth in the labor supply”.

It is worth noting that this paper was written during a time of Zero Interest Rate Policy (ZIRP) and may have been part of the justification for ZIRP.

The Fed proposes 2 reasons why higher population growth leads to higher R*

“Younger workers are more productive, thus increasing the returns from capital.

The expectation of a growing population leads firms to invest in more capital in anticipation of a larger workforce to utilize this capital for production.”

The basic outcome of the model is that the lower the population growth, the lower R* should be.

Miran is applying this model to the current situation, which is a substantially tighter immigration policy. He estimates that the changed immigration policy will lower population growth from 1% annually to 0.4%. Translating this into R* he says:

“A 1 percentage point drop in annual population growth can reduce r* by 0.6 percentage point. So, the expected decline in U.S. population growth equates to a nearly 0.4 percentage point drop in the neutral fed funds rate.”

His numbers appear to be roughly in line with the model posted above.

Miran’s largest deviation from the rest of the Fed

Miran’s largest point of differentiation from other Fed officials seems to be on how he views tariffs.

Most of the Fed is accounting for tariffs as an inflationary force which shows up in their estimates of inflation for the remainder of 2025 and 2026. So for them, tariffs are not impacting R* but since the Fed Funds target rate is some composite of inflation and R*, the net result is that most of the Fed are targeting a higher rate as a result of tariffs.

Miran has 2 views on tariffs that differ from the majority:

  1. He does not see tariffs as materially inflationary
  2. He sees the revenue side of tariffs as reducing R*

Per his speech:

“The Congressional Budget Office estimates tariff revenue could reduce the federal budget deficit by over $380 billion per year over the coming decade. This is a significant swing in the supply–demand balance for loanable funds, as national borrowing declines by a comparable amount. A 1 percentage point change in the deficit-to-GDP (gross domestic product) ratio moves r* by nearly four tenths of a percentage point, according to the average of estimates summarized by Rachel and Summers. This 1.3 percent of GDP change in national saving reduces the neutral rate by half a percentage point.”

The concept that lower deficit-to-GDP reduces R* makes sense to me directionally. It essentially improves the credit of the government so the demanded interest rate would be lower.

As for the magnitude of impact, Miran seems to be basing it on this historical data/model, which was cited in his speech.

The revenue from tariffs is quite easily measured on a backward basis. Forward tariff revenue is a bit harder to calculate as tariffs may cause behavioral spending shifts.

Similarly, we will not know the inflationary impacts of tariffs until more data comes out.

As time reveals more data on each point, the gap between Miran and the other Fed officials will likely close over time. It is just unknown at this point in which direction it will close.

Summing up the forward Fed

Miran’s approach emphasizes changes to R* itself which was previously a largely untouched figure. Most of the adjustments Miran makes to R* are in the negative direction, meaning a lower equilibrant real interest rate.

To the extent Miran persuades other Fed officials of his methodology or other like-minded individuals simply join the Fed via future appointments, I would anticipate a general downward movement of the Fed Funds rate.

Interest rate movements impact just about every financial instrument but some stand to benefit far more than others. In a follow-up article, we will discuss the individual securities we believe are best positioned for the environment.

data.title: Steelcase Continues To Justify A Bullish Outlook As Its Merger With HNI Nears
data.description:
Before the market opened on September 25th, the management team at Steelcase Inc. (NYSE:SCS) announced financial results for the second quarter of the firm's 2026 fiscal year. Although revenue and adjusted earnings per share came in well above what analysts were hoping to see, the stock price of the company barely budged. But that can be chalked up to the fact that it is tied heavily to HNI Corporation (NYSE:HNI), which is in the process of buying it out. Because of this relationship, upside for the company is limited from this point on. But seeing as how HNI Corporation makes for an attractive prospect and considering that buying Steelcase is tantamount to purchasing HNI Corporation at a discount, I would argue that the business still makes for an attractive Buy candidate, just like I did when I wrote about both firms in August of this year.

A great quarter

For the most part, I would say that the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a pretty solid time for shareholders of Steelcase. During the quarter, the company reported revenue of $897.1 million. That's an increase of 4.8% compared to the $855.8 million the business reported one year earlier. The revenue reported by management ended up being an impressive $23.5 million above what analysts were hoping to see. This growth in sales was driven almost entirely by organic revenue expansion of around 4%, with order growth expanding 6% on an organic basis. According to management, the greatest growth came in its international operations, with revenue expanding by 13% as organic revenue grew by 8%. This was attributable to strong demand coming out of India, China, and the UK, all of which more than offset weakness in France and Germany.

Primarily, the expansion that the company has seen came from large corporate customers. That was true not only internationally, but when it came to the Americas as well. Organic revenue in the Americas expanded by 3%, pushing up revenue by 3% on a year-over-year basis. Organic order growth was even stronger at 8% because of the aforementioned customers. Management specified that

business leaders are making investments in their workplaces as they bring their employees together, and they are turning to Steelcase for research-driven solutions that support connection, creativity and performance.

What I found to be particularly interesting was the firm's discussion, in its earnings call, regarding tariffs. According to management, the company had previously increased prices on its customers to reflect not only general inflation but also tariffs. They specified that these price increases have offset tariffs entirely, but they have not yet accounted for the tariffs that have negatively affected the business on backlog that was existing before they were implemented. That's because that backlog was based on pre-tariff prices. In addition to this, management said that the price increases have been enough that they might even be able to pick up "a little margin," meaning that they have increased prices more than what tariffs and broader inflation have called for. The fact that the company saw such strong sales in light of these meaningful price hikes is encouraging.

The bottom line for the company improved also. While earnings per share dropped from $0.53 last year to $0.29 this year, taking net profits down from $63.1 million to $35 million, the picture looks different if we look at adjusted earnings per share. Earnings per share ended up missing forecasts by $0.04, while adjusted earnings per share of $0.45 beat out analysts’ expectations by $0.09. That compared favorably to the $0.39 per share reported for the second quarter of 2025 and took adjusted net profits up from about $46.3 million to $53.9 million on a year-over-year basis.

To be clear, these adjusted calculations are based on my own numbers. While management reports adjusted earnings per share, they don't report adjusted net profits, so you need to merely multiply the outstanding shares of the company by the adjusted earnings per share to get a number that is comparable. Rounding errors can create a little uncertainty here, but nothing material.

Other profitability metrics for the company were largely positive. The one exception to this was operating cash flow, which plummeted from $108.1 million to $58.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a surge from $44.5 million to $74.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded nicely from $89.5 million to $99.6 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Although net profits and operating cash flows are both down, revenue and all of the other profitability metrics for the company improved year over year. This is fantastic to see and helps to illustrate that the second quarter on its own was not a blip on the radar.

Management has not really provided any detailed guidance for the rest of the year. But if we project out results based on what the company has achieved so far for this year, we would get adjusted net profits of $157.6 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $204.4 million, and EBITDA of $287 million. However, the performance of the business is not terribly significant anymore. After all, by the end of this calendar year, the business will be merging with HNI Corporation in a cash and stock deal valued at about $2.2 billion.

In my previous article about this transaction, I discussed the structure of the deal and its appeal. If everything goes according to plan, shareholders of Steelcase will receive $7.20 in cash for each share of stock that they own, as well as 0.2192 shares of HNI for each share. Right now, this translates to a purchase price of $16.97 compared to the $16.72 that shares of the company are currently trading at as of this writing. That's an upside of about 1.5%.

Normally, I would consider this to be unappealing. There are plenty of better ways that investors can allocate capital to get 1.5% over the span of a few months. However, because of the stock component of the transaction, the picture looks different. In essence, picking up Steelcase is like picking up HNI Corporation at a discount. And based on my own calculations, I would consider HNI Corporation to be an attractive business.

In my previous article about the two companies merging, I covered the most recent data that was provided regarding HNI Corporation. No new data has come out from HNI Corporation since then. But in the chart above, you can see financial performance for it over the last three fiscal years and for the first half of its current fiscal year compared to the same time last year. Assuming that the rest of this year continues on in a positive way like it has, we would be looking at net profits for it of $161.6 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $288.1 million, and EBITDA of $341.1 million.

Using these estimates, as well as historical results from last year, we can see in the chart above how HNI Corporation is currently valued. We can also see the same thing for Steelcase based on my previous calculations and historical results for the 2025 fiscal year. If we use the forward estimates for both firms, then we can see that the companies are priced very similarly. So there isn't some huge imbalance here in terms of valuation. Since I see HNI Corporation as an attractive prospect, I must by definition consider Steelcase to be appealing as well, especially in light of this strong second quarter release.

There is a caveat here, however, that needs to be taken into consideration. Because of the cash and stock nature of the merger, both upside and downside for shareholders of Steelcase will be less severe than it would be for HNI Corporation. This can be seen by the chart below, which illustrates how much upside or downside the stock would experience based on the upside or downside that HNI Corporation sees. This has major implications depending on your overall thesis. If you expect a breakout for shares of HNI Corporation in the near term, it would actually be better to purchase it as opposed to owning Steelcase. But on the other hand, if you expect the upside to be more modest or you expect potential economic weakness that would push shares of HNI Corporation down, then buy up Steelcase.

The way I view this is that if you expect market conditions to warrant some pessimism in the near term, or even for the stocks to trade flat moving forward, Steelcase is the more sensible approach. But if you expect a breakout from HNI Corporation for one reason or another, both companies are attractive, but HNI Corporation is more so.

Takeaway

Although upside associated with Steelcase will be far more limited absent a major change in the share price of HNI Corporation moving forward, I view both companies in a favorable light at this time. Fundamentally, the picture is rather appealing right now. And both companies are priced at levels that I would consider to be attractive. But of course, which one makes the most sense will depend on your view of the direction of HNI Corporation moving forward. Regardless, I believe that both companies deserve a Buy rating at this time.

data.title: Housing: 3 Things To Expect In 2026
data.description:
We are on the cusp of commencing the fourth quarter. 2025 has been a good year for equity investors to date. It has been a much more challenging year for homeowners and prospective sellers. Home prices are holding up well in some regions of the country with low inventories, like parts of the Midwest and Northeast.

However, in many major metros, it has become quite apparent that the housing bubble has started to pop. And unfortunately, this housing downturn seems like it is in its early innings, given housing affordability remains near historical lows. This is a topic I have returned to often over the past few quarters. The reason is simple. First, housing-related activity accounts for 15% to 18% of GDP. In addition, few things outside accelerating job losses undermine consumer confidence like falling home prices.

What will the housing sector look like in 2026? Here are three likely trends in store for next year around housing.

1. Much Higher Foreclosure Rates

Despite falling home prices in many metros, the current U.S. home foreclosure rate in the first half of this year was only 0.13%. This reflects a couple of things. The first is there was real mortgage reform following the Housing Bust that helped trigger the Great Financial Crisis. Liar Loans and no-income-verification mortgages are things of the past. Most mortgage loans these days are made with at least 20% down payments. Some 40% of all homes have no mortgage balances against them at all.

Of course, we have different issues to worry about during this potential housing bust. It is not only high-profile federal officials who might have been taking mortgage loans on properties by listing them as primary residences when, in reality, they were second homes and/or investment properties. Other applicants can garner lower mortgage rates by doing this too, and it has not been policed as thoroughly as it should have been by lenders.

This is one reason Airbnb homes blossomed during COVID and the immediate aftermath of the pandemic. Many of these homes are now being put on the market, as demand for these homes has dropped significantly since the days of revenge travel immediately following the end of the lockdowns.

The other issue that is starting to become prominent is what noted housing analyst Melody Wright has dubbed Government Subprime. These are loans made by the FHA and VA. These are low- or no-down-payment mortgages. They are the first to go underwater when housing prices decline. In addition, home sellers far outnumber home buyers currently across the nation.

This will be even more problematic in the quarters ahead. For nearly four years following COVID, the government engaged in beyond generous taxpayer-financed loss mitigation efforts around FHA mortgages, which total just less than 8 million mortgages.

Most of these efforts finally come to an end at the end of this month. Some of these efforts were truly extraordinary. Homeowners who couldn't make payments were given forbearance freely. The FHA just tacked the missed mortgage payments onto the outstanding loan balance. Many FHA mortgage holders have had this done multiple times. In regions of the country where housing prices haven't risen in the past few years or have started to fall, this means many of these mortgages already have negative equity.

As these programs come to an end, more borrowers will go into foreclosure proceedings. Even with these COVID-driven programs, the mortgage delinquency rate hit 10.57% at the end of Q2. VA loans, which account for just over 10% of overall mortgage originations, had a 4.7% delinquency rate at the end of 2024.

Given how long it takes to get through the foreclosure process, investors can expect a notable uptick in foreclosures on the market by the second quarter of 2026.

2. Average Home Prices Accelerate Downward

Additional supply from increasing foreclosures is the last thing the housing market needs right now. The market is also facing a longer-term headwind as 6,000 Baby Boomers are passing away daily, and the largest generational cohort in American history owns just over 40% of residential housing stock in the country. A lot of those houses will hit the market to settle inheritances over the next decade. And this is with existing home inventory already at 4.6 months of supply. That is nearly triple their level in early 2022, before the Federal Reserve started to raise rates in March of that year.

Since 2012, at the end of the previous housing bust, the average new home price has been significantly above the average existing home price. This makes sense given a new house has a new roof, new appliances, usually lower HOAs in new communities, and lower insurance rates. In June, new home prices traded at a 6.5% discount to existing home prices, and in July, a 4% discount. This has never happened since records began being kept.

Now, it is true that August new home sales rose over 20% from July, in a reading that was posted on Wednesday. However, as Wolf Street went into great detail around, the entire sales spike was in the South. It is also likely a one-off and likely to be revised sharply lower in future revisions. Much like recent BLS monthly jobs data.

Completed new single-home inventory is at its highest level since 2009, up 18% from August 2024 and a whopping 62% rise from August 2019, just before the pandemic.

New home inventory across all phases of construction rose 3.4% from August 2024 and 51% from August 2019. Given average new home prices are under the average existing home prices, current new home inventory levels, and more supply that will hit the market in the quarters ahead, it is difficult to see how average existing home prices in most regions of the country don't accelerate their downward slide in 2026.

3. Naked Swimmers Get Exposed

One of the major changes in the mortgage markets since the Great Financial Crisis is that major banks hold a much lower percentage of overall mortgage debt now (around $13 trillion overall). In their place, government-run entities like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have boosted their mortgage holdings percentage-wise. As have hedge funds and private equity concerns.

The challenge with this distribution is twofold. First, these entities do not have access to trillions of low-cost consumer deposits like a JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM). In addition, private firms do not have to submit SEC filings quarterly like public companies. Therefore, it is much harder to monitor what is happening with their balance sheets and credit loss provisions.

However, it should be noted that Fannie Mae added nearly $950 million to its loan loss provisions in Q2. This could be the canary in the coal mine on how delinquencies and defaults are expected to play out in the mortgage markets in the coming quarters. That is why I think some naked swimmers will become more apparent in 2026, with some entities taking some high-profile losses on mortgage debt. Seven states had over five percent of mortgage holders underwater at the end of the second quarter. That number will grow in the months ahead.

For these reasons, I think the deterioration in the housing market is going to climb up investors' worry lists in 2026 and have increasingly negative impacts on the U.S. economy.

data.title: Home Depot: Recent Earnings Show Growth Has Stagnated
data.description:
Pent-up demand is often a double-edged sword for investors and valuation. While obviously the idea of companies having record or historical earnings is appealing, the market has tended to overvalue companies' potential growth prospects that saw significant earnings growth during the COVID years.

There are a few companies that benefited more from the lockdowns than The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE:HD). The industry leader benefited from people who were staying at home during the lockdowns, upgrading their houses, people who began to work remotely on a more permanent basis, outfitting their residence for their profession, and then also from the historic rise in housing prices and an impressive number of new homes bought coming out of the pandemic.

Home Depot was one of the better-performing companies coming out of the pandemic.

Home Depot offered investors total returns of 98.66 percent coming out of the pandemic and COVID-related lockdowns, while the S&P 500 (SPY) rose 52.40 percent during this same time period.

Still, the retailer has underperformed for the last 2 years.

While the S&P 500 has offered investors total returns of 51.33 percent since September 2023, Home Depot has offered total returns of 29.59 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about this industry leader in February of 2025, and I rated the company a strong sell. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating. Home Depot has only grown earnings and revenues marginally since 2022, and the retailer's recent quarter presented a mixed picture for investors. There are also multiple signs that consumers are being more cautious with prices remaining high and labor markets weakening, the company looks overvalued using multiple metrics.

Home Depot reported second-quarter earnings per share GAAP Actual $4.58 and revenues actual of $45.28 billion, missing earnings per share expectations of $4.61 and revenues forecasts of nearly $45.5 billion. While management reported a 4.9 percent increase in sales and a 1 percent rise in sales for the comparable time period, the retailer also stated that earnings per share fell on a year-to-year basis by .3 percent. The company reiterated full-year guidance for sales growth of 2 percent and comparable sales growth of 1 percent. Management expects a 2 percent decline in adjusted diluted earnings per share on a year-to-year basis.

Home Depot has been unable to grow the company's 12-month trailing earnings since the end of 2022, when management reported that full-year EPS for that time period was $16.69. The company's quarterly earnings growth rate has also been declining for 8 of the last 11 quarters.

Home Depot's net margins have also been in sequential decline for 10 straight quarters now. The company's net margins fell from 9.71 percent last year to 8.86 percent in the second quarter of this year. While Home Depot reported a notable rise in revenues on a quarter-to-quarter and year-to-year basis, the company reported falling earnings primarily because of margin compression as consumers were clearly being more cautious and trading down.

Home Depot also faces a number of headwinds moving forward. Consumer credit card debt levels have reached record highs, the housing market is slowing, and people are also working less from home now, which should continue to lower demand for do-it-yourself projects. The company also cited higher interest rates and increasing costs as reasons why projects have slowed. Powell also remained firm in his position that lowering rates too aggressively has significant risks, since the Fed chair continues to maintain that inflationary pressures and growth challenges are both significant concerns for the Central Bank right now.

This is why I believe Home Depot looks overvalued at 28.08x expected forward GAAP earnings. The retailer's median 5-year average valuation is 22.51x projected forward GAAP earnings, and the industry leader also reported record earnings per share in fiscal year 2023, so comparisons are more difficult for Home Depot.

Home Depot is only expected to grow revenues at 3-5 percent over the next several years, and the company's GAAP earnings are being projected to grow at a mid to high-single-digit rate during this same time period. These estimates are also likely overly optimistic since the retailer also faces a more challenging environment now, with growth slowing, rates still high, and some political uncertainty as well, with the president's recent actions using tariffs.

All investment theses have risks, and I believe the President is likely to replace Powell with a more dovish chairman next year, so rates could be brought down more aggressively in 2026, potentially positively impacting the housing market in particular. Home Depot's revenues and cash flow also remain solid, and although the company's growth has stalled, management maintains a good amount of flexibility. The rate of inflation has slowed slightly as well, although prices remain high.

Well-known companies don't always make good investments, and Home Depot has not been able to drive any consistent or substantive earnings growth over the last 2 years since the company's record year in fiscal year 2023. While the retailer has been able to maintain market share and the company's revenues continue to hold up, Home Depot's slowing growth rate and falling margins should concern investors.

data.title: Southern Copper: Low Costs And Strong Projects, But Priced For Perfection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Copper remains, in my opinion, the most solid story among commodities. The structural demand linked to electrification, data centers, and energy transition does not find a correlation in the supply, which remains restricted by decreasing laws, regulatory delays, and social conflicts in producing countries.

In this context, Southern Copper (NYSE:SCCO) is a privileged player: during Q2 2025, it had net profits of $973 MM (+2.4% YoY), with a net margin of 31.9% and an adjusted EBITDA of $1.79 B, equivalent to a margin of 58.7%. The quarterly production of copper was at 239,980 tons, with a net cash cost of just $0.70/lb, one of the lowest in the industry.

At the same time, SCCO is also advancing with major projects that could transform its scale: Tía María in Peru (120,000 tons annually since 2027), Michiquillay (225,000 tons with a useful life of more than 25 years) and El Arco in Mexico, considered a world-class repository with more than 1.2 billion tons of sulfide reserves.

However, the main problem I observe is that the market is already discounting a large part of this strength. The stock is traded with very demanding multiples: P/E FWD 23.8x and EV/EBITDA FWD 13.3x, premiums of more than 40-50% compared to the sector. The P/B FWD 8.3x and P/CF FWD 21.1x reflect a price that assumes perfect execution in projects.

At the same time, the risks of permits, social tensions, and volatility in the global copper market are no less, as was demonstrated in September by the suspension of Grasberg by Freeport (FCX), which pushed futures up overnight.

Therefore, although the fundamentals of SCCO are unbeatable and justify its position as one of the most efficient and profitable producers in the world, my position is to hold: keep the stock in the portfolio for its strategic role and its ability to generate cash, but without seeing a broad short-term upside to these levels of valuation.

Sectoral context

The global copper market in 2025 is under strain. Reuters reported last month that "Chile’s Codelco will lower its 2025 copper production target after an accident at its El Teniente mine, cutting around 33,000 tonnes from its output"

While in Peru, the world’s second largest producer, production is maintained on a plateau that limits the capacity to respond to higher prices.

In addition, this month Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) announced that it could not meet deliveries from Grasberg, its main mine in Indonesia, invoking force majeure and leaving the market without part of its production.

These episodes show how fragile global supply is and explain recent price volatility. SCCO, with stable operations and low costs, benefits from this backdrop but is also not free of local risks and social conflicts.

Recent results and projects

Q2 2025 again showed why SCCO is a key player in the global copper board. With an EBITDA of $1.79 B and a margin of 58.7%, the company not only delivered solid results but did so in the midst of a turbulent market. Net income was $973 MM (+2.4% YoY), and all this with a net cash cost of only $0.70/lb, a figure that places the company among the most efficient on the planet, allowing it to navigate global volatility with a level of security that few can match.

But what really catches the eye is not just the numbers of the present, but what comes next. SCCO is betting heavily on projects that, if implemented, could change the scale of the company. In Peru, Tía María represents a kind of "difficult promise": after years of social opposition, it begins to advance and could add 120,000 tons annually since 2027. It is not a simple project, but if it succeeds, it will be a symbol that the company can grow even in complex social scenarios.

Very close appears Michiquillay, a project that is spoken of almost like a "mine for a whole generation." Its estimated production of 225,000 tons per year and a useful life of more than 25 years make it a true pillar for SCCO’s future in the Andean region. And it does not come alone: The Chancas, with a planned start-up by 2030 and an annual contribution of nearly 130,000 tons, continues this growth story, ensuring that the company does not depend on a single project to sustain its expansion.

In Mexico, the absolute protagonist is El Arco. More than a project, it is a dream in my opinion: a deposit with more than 1.2 billion tons of reserves that could add about 200,000 tons annually in its first stage. It’s not a smooth road ahead, as the investment is huge and environmental permits can take time, but if you go further, it will redefine the weight of Southern Copper in the industry.

If this entire pipeline succeeds in materializing, SCCO would be adding more than 500,000 tons annually towards the end of the decade. That means you would be playing in the same league as Codelco or Freeport-McMoRan, not only for volume but also for stability: they are projects of several decades of life, which help guarantee continuity in a global market where copper is increasingly scarce.

Valuation, growth, and profitability

When I look at the valuation of SCCO it is clear to me that the market already recognizes a premium, and does so by paying dearly. The P/E FWD of 23.8x is 45% above the industry median and still 14% higher than its own five-year average. The same is true for the EV/EBITDA FWD of 13.3x, which reflects a premium higher than 50% compared to its peers.

If I go further, the 8.3x P/B FWD and 21.1x P/CF FWD make it clear that investors are assuming, with almost no margin of error, that the company will successfully execute all the projects it has in its portfolio. In other words, SCCO is listed for what it is: a first-class company but with very high expectations.

Growth, on the other hand, has been strong. In the last twelve months, revenues increased by 13% YoY, EBITDA rose by 20.8%, and diluted EPS by 31.7%, figures that surpass the sector.

Looking ahead, the projections are not as explosive, but they remain solid: revenues +6.2% and EPS +15.2% per annum, with a 3-5 year CAGR of EPS of 14.3%. In my reading, this means that Southern Copper is not only coming from an exceptional year, but has what it takes to sustain a path of sustained expansion.

The key to why the market pays these premiums is profitability. With an EBITDA margin of 56.9%, a net margin of 30.4% and an ROE of 39%, SCCO plays directly in another league. Few mining companies can show such strong numbers, and that gives them a competitive advantage that goes beyond the price of copper in the short term.

Even the FCF margin of 28% makes it clear that it has the capacity to finance its own projects without relying too much on debt or the capital market.

Risks

However, not everything is about strength, as the risks exist and we cannot ignore them.

In the case of Peru and Mexico, we must emphasize that large projects still depend on environmental permits and social acceptance. It is enough for a project to be delayed or for a local conflict to resurface to cause plans to be postponed for years.

In the US, the July episode made it very clear: although the announced tariffs did not apply to primary copper "the tariff does not apply to raw forms of copper (e.g., ores, concentrates and mattes)", futures on COMEX fell by more than 20% within a matter of hours.

This volatility reminds us that this industry is exposed to geopolitics as well as geology.

Conclusion

There is no doubt that SCCO is one of the most profitable mining companies in the world, with extraordinary margins and a pipeline that can add up to more than 500,000 tons in the next decade.

Its role as a low-cost producer and its place in copper, a critical metal for the energy transition, justify that the market should reward it with higher multiples.

However, that premium already reflects much of its potential, and enforcement and regulatory risks remain.

Therefore, my current position is to hold: keep the stock in the portfolio, relying on its ability to generate cash and its strategic position, but without adding additional exposure to this price. For me, the best entry opportunity will be when the market goes through a correction to bring the stock to a more reasonable valuation point.

data.title: Pfizer: Invest For The Yield, Not The Weight-Loss Drug Chase
data.description:
Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE) has struggled since the slump in Covid vaccine demand, leading the biopharma to chase buying growth. The company bought a weight-loss biotech without even having a Phase 3 drug trial, which feels like Pfizer throwing away money to chase growth. My investment thesis remains Bullish on the drug company trading closer to the lows with a 7% dividend yield.

Potential $7 Billion Deal

Pfizer announced a deal to acquire Metsera, Inc. (MTSR) for $47.50 per share in cash, valuing the weight-loss company at $4.9 billion. The company only recently went public pricing the IPO at just $18.

The deal is already 164% above the IPO price. In addition, Metsera shareholders will receive up to an additional $22.50 per share through contingent value rights (CVRs) tied to future milestones pushing the deal valuation up to a potential $7.2 billion.

The CVRs aren't difficult milestones with Metsera only required to obtain approvals of the MET-097i and MET-233i drugs in order to collect the $22.50 cash value. Investors would only need the Phase 3 start of the drugs in order to collect a $5 share cash payment, eliminating any requirement for these drugs to launch successfully to a potentially crowded weight-loss drug market.

The small biotech is focused on GLP-1 drugs. MET-097i is a weekly and monthly injectable GLP-1 receptor agonist in Phase 2 development, and MET-233i is a monthly amylin analog candidate being evaluated as a monotherapy in combination with MET-097i in a Phase 1 trial.

Metsera also has two oral GLP-1 RA candidates expected to begin clinical trials imminently and additional preclinical nutrient-stimulated hormone therapeutics. The drug pipeline is really just different tests for the 2 primary obesity drug candidates.

The MET-097i Phase 2 data showed up to an 11.3% weight loss after day 85 (~12 weeks) on the weekly dosing. The 1.2 mg dose continues to show increasing weight loss after 12 weeks.

The drug did show a high level of nausea and vomiting at the 1.2 mg weekly dose. Metsera also has data showing the drug continued to result in a weight loss of 14.1% after 16 weeks when shifting to a monthly dose of 4.8 mg.

The Metsera weight-loss drugs have some promising weight-loss metrics, but the company doesn't expect to start a Phase 3 trial until sometime next year. The acquisition is expected to close in Q4, but Pfizer will be very late to the weight-loss market with this drug.

On the acquisition call, Pfizer's Chief Strategy & Innovation Officer pinned the drug launch timeline as follows:

Subject to clinical and regulatory success, we anticipate Metsera's pipeline will deliver a series of launches beginning in the 2028, '29 time frame that would accelerate our growth trajectory following our major loss of exclusives.

The company already faces competition from Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) and Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) with approved weight-loss drugs while smaller biotechs have possibly more impressive weight-loss drugs in development. Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (VKTX) has an oral pill with a 12.2% weight loss after 13%, again questioning whether an injectable from Metsera in 2028 and beyond will be impressive.

Analysts at Leerink Partners forecast that the Metsera drugs are differentiated enough to offer the potential for $5 billion in peak sales. The obesity drug market has already been forecast by Morgan Stanley to reach a $150+ billion market size by 2035, so maybe Pfizer has the potential to capture some market share without a leading drug.

Earlier this year, Pfizer scrapped its experimental weight-loss pill danuglipron over concerns about potential liver damage. One has to wonder if the biopharma should've just moved on from the sector.

Tasty Yield

Pfizer has bounced off the bottom recently, but the stock still has a massive dividend yield of 7.1%. The yield has surged as the biopharma has failed to generate growth due to collapsing COVID vaccine sales.

The company continues to hike 2025 EPS targets due to solid revenue beats and a plan to deliver over $7 billion in net cost savings by 2027. Pfizer is now forecasting an EPS of $2.90 to $3.10 with a consensus analyst target of $3.07.

The biggest issue facing the biopharma industry is the patent cliff, which the business faces with major drugs in the next few years. The consensus of analysts' estimates has sales slipping to below $55 billion by 2029.

Clearly, Pfizer could use a product to fill that revenue hole in the next few years, but drugs hitting the market in 2028/29 appear late to the market and it comes off like the biopharma is chasing the current hot product.

The stock is cheap trading below 10x earnings and our prior research highlighted the appeal in the biopharma growing cancer drugs where worldwide sales grew from $4.0 billion to $4.4 billion YoY. If anything, Pfizer seems to have a strong position in oncology and vaccines and chasing weight-loss drugs appears to be a lack of focus.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Pfizer isn't a Buy due to this acquisition. The large biopharma seems late to the weight-loss drug game and could end up spending $7+ billion on a drug lineup that might not produce material revenues in the future.

Investors can collect the 7% yield while results continue to improve while any weight-loss drug sales would be a bonus to future results.
